title: Performance & Deployment
content:
## 🚀 React Performance & Deployment A fast app retains users. A slow one loses them. This lesson covers the rendering optimization techniques and deployment strategies used in production React applications. --- ## 1️⃣ Why React Re-renders React re-renders a component when: 1. Its **state** changes (`useState`, `useReducer`) 2. Its **props** change 3. Its **parent** re-renders (even if this component's props didn't change) 4. Its **context** value changes **Goal:** Prevent unnecessary re-renders using memoization. --- ## 2️⃣ `React.memo` — Memoize Components ```javascript // Without memo: re-renders whenever parent re-renders function TaskItem({ task, onComplete }) { return
  • onComplete(task.id)}>{task.title}
    • } // With memo: only re-renders if task or onComplete CHANGE const TaskItem = React.memo(function TaskItem({ task, onComplete }) { return
  • onComplete(task.id)}>{task.title}
    • }) ``` **Important:** Pair with `useCallback` for handler props, otherwise new function references break memoization. --- ## 3️⃣ `useMemo` and `useCallback` for Performance ```javascript function TaskList({ tasks, filter }) { // Only recalculate filteredTasks when tasks or filter changes const filteredTasks = useMemo(() => { return tasks.filter(t => t.priority === filter) }, [tasks, filter]) // Stable function reference — prevents TaskItem from re-rendering const handleComplete = useCallback((id) => { completeTask(id) }, []) return filteredTasks.map(t => ) } ``` **Key Point:** `useMemo` and `useCallback` have a cost too (memory, comparison overhead). Only use them when you've measured a real performance problem. --- ## 4️⃣ Code Splitting with `React.lazy` Load components only when needed — reduces initial bundle size: ```javascript import { lazy, Suspense } from 'react' // The Dashboard component's code only downloads when needed const Dashboard = lazy(() => import('./pages/Dashboard')) const Analytics = lazy(() => import('./pages/Analytics')) function App() { return ( Loading...}> } /> } /> ) } ``` --- ## 5️⃣ Virtualization — Large Lists Rendering 10,000 list items is slow. **Virtualization** renders only the items visible in the viewport: ```bash npm install react-window ``` ```javascript import { FixedSizeList } from 'react-window' function VirtualList({ items }) { const Row = ({ index, style }) => (
    {items[index].name}
    // 'style' MUST be applied ) return ( {Row} ) } ``` Only renders ~12 items at a time instead of all 10,000. --- ## 6️⃣ Image Optimization ```html ``` --- ## 7️⃣ Web Vitals & Performance Metrics Google's **Core Web Vitals** measure real user experience: | Metric | Meaning | Good | |--------|---------|------| | **LCP** (Largest Contentful Paint) | Time to render largest element | < 2.5s | | **FID** (First Input Delay) | Time to respond to first interaction | < 100ms | | **CLS** (Cumulative Layout Shift) | Unexpected layout shifts | < 0.1 | | **FCP** (First Contentful Paint) | Time to first visible content | < 1.8s | | **TTI** (Time to Interactive) | Time until fully interactive | < 3.8s | Measure with Chrome DevTools → Lighthouse tab. --- ## 8️⃣ Deploying to Vercel Vercel is the easiest way to deploy Next.js and React apps: ```bash npm install -g vercel vercel login vercel deploy # Deploy current directory ``` Or: Connect your GitHub repo on vercel.com → deploys automatically on every push. **Environment variables:** Set in Vercel dashboard → Settings → Environment Variables. --- ## 9️⃣ Environment Variables ```bash # .env.local (never commit to git!) NEXT_PUBLIC_API_URL=https://api.example.com # Exposed to browser (NEXT_PUBLIC_ prefix) DATABASE_URL=mongodb+srv://... # Server-only (no prefix) JWT_SECRET=ultrasecretkey123 ``` Access in code: ```javascript const apiUrl = process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_API_URL // Browser-safe const dbUrl = process.env.DATABASE_URL // Server only ``` --- ## 🔟 Build Optimization Checklist - [ ] Enable production build (`next build` or `vite build`) - [ ] Enable tree-shaking (automatic in Vite/webpack) - [ ] Lazy-load large components - [ ] Virtualize long lists - [ ] Optimize images (WebP format, lazy loading) - [ ] Enable gzip/Brotli compression on server - [ ] Set proper cache headers for static assets - [ ] Run Lighthouse audit (aim for 90+ scores) --- ## 💡 Summary | Optimization | When to Use | |---|---| | `React.memo` | Prevent re-renders of expensive pure components | | `useMemo` | Cache expensive computed values | | `useCallback` | Stable function references for memoized children | | Code splitting | Large apps with many routes/features | | Virtualization | Lists with 1000+ items | | Lazy images | Images below the fold | | WebP format | All user-uploaded or static images | | Lighthouse | After deployment to measure real performance |

questions.question_text: What is the purpose of 'minification' in the build process?
options: To make the code more readable
To reduce file size for faster loading
questions.explanation: Minification removes unnecessary characters from code without changing its functionality, resulting in smaller files that load faster over the network.

options: Breaking code into two separate files
Dynamic loading of code chunks only when needed to reduce initial bundle size
questions.explanation: Vite and Webpack handle this by creating separate .js files for different routes or large libraries.

questions.question_text: Which React component works with React.lazy to show a loading state during a split chunk download?
questions.explanation: Suspense takes a 'fallback' prop (usually a spinner).

questions.question_text: What is the benefit of 'Memoization' (React.memo) for components?
options: It prevents a component from re-rendering if its props haven't changed
It saves them to the database
It hides them from the user
questions.explanation: Great for large lists or complex components used frequently.

questions.question_text: What does a 'Production Build' do to your code?
options: Minifies code, removes comments, and optimizes assets for the smallest possible size
questions.explanation: Production builds are much faster for the user to download.

questions.question_text: Which command is standard for creating a production build in a Vite/React app?
questions.explanation: The output is usually placed in a '/dist' or '/build' folder.

options: Automatically removing unused code from the final bundle
questions.explanation: Modern tools only include the functions you actually `import`.

questions.question_text: Where is a common place to host a modern React frontend for free?

options: A way to store sensitive keys (like API URLs) that change between development and production
A secret password for the user
questions.explanation: Never commit sensitive .env values to GitHub!

questions.question_text: What is the purpose of 'Lighthouse' in Chrome DevTools?
options: To audit the Performance, Accessibility, and SEO of your website
questions.explanation: Aim for high scores to ensure a premium user experience.

options: Sending a pre-rendered HTML page from the server to the browser for better SEO and perceived speed
Rendering code on the user's phone
questions.explanation: Frameworks like Next.js are popular for SSR.

options: A website that is always updating
A website that can be installed on a home screen and work offline like an app
questions.explanation: PWAs use 'Service Workers' to cache files offline.

questions.question_text: Which attribute on a <script> tag helps improve page load speed?
questions.explanation: These prevent the script from blocking the initial HTML rendering.

questions.question_text: How do you avoid 'Large Component Trees' becoming slow?
options: Component splitting and keeping state local as much as possible
questions.explanation: Only update what needs to be updated.

questions.question_text: What is 'Hydration' in the context of SSR?
options: The process of React 'attaching' its logic to static HTML already sent by the server
questions.explanation: Hydration makes the static page interactive.

questions.question_text: What does a 'Content Delivery Network' (CDN) do?
options: Distributes your website files across servers worldwide to serve them from the closest location to the user
questions.explanation: CDNs make your site load fast everywhere on Earth.
